phase_metadata.title: CSS Basics: Styling the Web
phase_metadata.description:
Introduction to Cascading Style Sheets (CSS), methods of adding
styles to HTML, and basic property manipulation for text and colors.

learning_objectives:
Understand the role
of CSS in separating content from presentation

Implement CSS using Inline,
Internal, and External methods

Master CSS syntax including selectors,
properties, and values
Apply colors using names, Hex, RGB, and HSL values

Manipulate text appearance
using font and alignment properties
prerequisites:
Solid understanding
of HTML tags and attributes (Phases 1-4)

Ability to create and link files in
a code editor
introduction:
HTML provides the structure (the skeleton),
but CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) provides the beauty (the skin, clothes, and
makeup). CSS allows you to control the color, font, size, and layout of your
HTML elements. By learning CSS, you move from building plain documents to
designing professional, visually engaging websites.

concept_explanation.what_is_css:
CSS is a stylesheet language used to describe the presentation
of a document written in HTML. It allows you to apply styles globally across
your entire website from a single file.
concept_explanation.inclusion_methods:
There are three
ways to add CSS: 1. Inline (inside the HTML tag), 2. Internal (inside a  tag in
the ), and 3. External (in a separate .css file linked via ). External is the
industry standard.
concept_explanation.css_syntax:
CSS works by selecting an HTML element and
then declaring a property (what you want to change) and a value (how you want to
change it).
concept_explanation.cascading:
The 'Cascading' in CSS means that styles follow a
hierarchy. If multiple styles conflict, the browser decides which one to apply
based on specificity and order.
concept_explanation.color_systems:
CSS supports multiple ways
to define color: Names (red), Hexadecimal (#ff0000), RGB (rgb(255,0,0)), and HSL
(hue, saturation, lightness).

breakdown_of_syntax.selector: The HTML element you want to style (e.g., h1, p, div).
breakdown_of_syntax.property:
The feature you want to change (e.g., color, font-size,
background-color).
breakdown_of_syntax.value:
The specific setting for that property (e.g.,
blue, 20px, #000).
breakdown_of_syntax.declaration:
The combination of property and value
(e.g., color: blue;).

rules_and_key_points.rule_1:
Every CSS declaration must end
with a semicolon (;).
rules_and_key_points.rule_2:
The property and value are separated by a
colon (:).
rules_and_key_points.rule_3:
CSS property names and values are case-insensitive, but
lowercase is the standard.
rules_and_key_points.rule_4:
External CSS files must not contain any
HTML tags like .
rules_and_key_points.rule_5:
Inline styles have the highest priority but are
hard to maintain; use them sparingly.
rules_and_key_points.rule_6:
Comments in CSS start with /*
and end with /.
rules_and_key_points.rule_7:
Hex colors consist of 6 characters (0-9, A-F)
preceded by a #.
rules_and_key_points.rule_8:
Font-family values with spaces (like 'Times New
Roman') must be in quotes.
rules_and_key_points.rule_9:
The 'background' property is a shorthand
for multiple background-related properties.
rules_and_key_points.rule_10:
Browsers read CSS from
top to bottom; later rules can override earlier ones.

code_example_1.title: Internal CSS and Text Styling
code_example_1.description:
Applying colors and
font properties within the HTML head.

code_example_2.title: External CSS (style.css)
code_example_2.description:
The professional way to style: separating the CSS into its own
file.

output:
Example 1 results in a light-gray page with a centered, underlined navy heading
and readable gray text. Example 2 (if applied to HTML) creates a header with a
background image and specific 'highlighted' text that is bold, dark green, and
has a yellow background.
step_by_step_dry_run:
Browser parses the HTML
and finds the  tag or .

The CSS engine identifies the 'body' selector and
applies a light-gray background to the whole page.

It finds the 'h1'
selector, changes its text color to navy, and centers it.

It hits the 'p'
selector and adjusts the font size to 18px.

If an inline style exists on a
specific tag, it overrides the general rules for that specific tag only.

The
final rendered page reflects the combination of all applied styles.

Not linking the external CSS file correctly (wrong path in href).
best_practices:
Use External CSS files for better organization and
performance.

Use meaningful property values (e.g., use 'rem' or 'px'
consistently).

Organize your CSS by grouping related styles (e.g., all header
styles together).

Avoid inline styles as they make HTML messy and hard to
update.

Always provide a fallback font (e.g., font-family: Arial,
sans-serif;).

faqs.question:
Which CSS method is the fastest for
the browser?
faqs.answer:
External CSS is generally best because the browser
caches the file, meaning it only downloads it once even if the user visits
multiple pages.

faqs.question:
What is the difference between 'color' and
'background-color'?
faqs.answer:
'color' changes the color of the text itself,
while 'background-color' changes the color of the box/area behind the text.

summary:
Phase 5 introduced the power of CSS. We learned the syntax
(selector/property/value), the three ways to link CSS to HTML, and how to
control basic visual elements like colors, fonts, and backgrounds. This is the
starting point for all web design.
key_takeaways:
CSS controls the look
and feel of a website.

External CSS is the preferred method for professional
sites.
CSS syntax consists of a selector and a declaration block.

Every
declaration needs a colon and a semicolon.

Hex, RGB, and HSL are common ways
to define colors.
Inline styles override internal and external styles.

Proper
indentation makes CSS readable for developers.
preview_next_topic:
In
Phase 6, we will learn the 'CSS Box Model'—the most important concept in web
layout, which defines how margins, borders, and padding work together.

questions_and_answers.question:
What are the three ways to add CSS to
an HTML document?
questions_and_answers.answer:
The three ways are: 1. Inline (using the style
attribute), 2. Internal (using the  tag in the ), and 3. External (using a  tag
to point to a .css file).

questions_and_answers.question: Explain the structure of a CSS rule.
questions_and_answers.answer:
A CSS rule consists of a
selector (the element to style) and a declaration block. Inside the block, you
have properties and values separated by colons, with each declaration ending in
a semicolon.

questions_and_answers.question:
Write the CSS
code to make all level-2 headings blue, centered, and using the 'Verdana'
font.

Create a CSS rule for a class named 'info-box' that has a light-yellow
background, a font size of 14px, and dark-gray text using an RGB value.
Identify the bug:  p { color: red font-size: 12px }
questions_and_answers.answer:
There is a
missing semicolon after 'red'. It should be 'color: red;' otherwise the browser
cannot distinguish where the first property ends and the next begins.

questions_and_answers.question:
A student linked their CSS
using . Why might the styles not appear?

Apply styles to a paragraph so that it has a background image
'bg.jpg', the text is bold, and the text is white.

Write a complete HTML document
that uses internal CSS to change the entire body background to a Hex color #222
and all text inside to HSL(0, 0%, 90%).
